# UC-14.4.6 · IoT Device Connectivity and Last-Seen Monitoring

## Description

Devices that stop reporting indicate failure, network issues, or tampering. Last-seen monitoring ensures fleet visibility and rapid response to outages.

## Value

Devices that stop reporting indicate failure, network issues, or tampering. Last-seen monitoring ensures fleet visibility and rapid response to outages.

## Implementation

Track last-seen per device from telemetry or heartbeat. Alert when device has not reported for >1 hour (tune per use case). Report on connectivity rate and devices with longest gap. Correlate with gateway health.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: IoT platform, MQTT/CoAP gateway logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Device heartbeat, last telemetry timestamp per device.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track last-seen per device from telemetry or heartbeat. Alert when device has not reported for >1 hour (tune per use case). Report on connectivity rate and devices with longest gap. Correlate with gateway health.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=iot sourcetype="iot:telemetry"
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen by device_id, gateway
| eval gap_sec=now()-last_seen
| where gap_sec > 3600
| table device_id, gateway, last_seen, gap_sec
```

Understanding this SPL

**IoT Device Connectivity and Last-Seen Monitoring** — Devices that stop reporting indicate failure, network issues, or tampering. Last-seen monitoring ensures fleet visibility and rapid response to outages.

Documented **Data sources**: Device heartbeat, last telemetry timestamp per device. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): IoT platform, MQTT/CoAP gateway logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: iot; **sourcetype**: iot:telemetry. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=iot, sourcetype="iot:telemetry". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by device_id, gateway** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **gap_sec** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where gap_sec > 3600` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **IoT Device Connectivity and Last-Seen Monitoring**): table device_id, gateway, last_seen, gap_sec


Step 3 — Validate
Compare device counts, last-seen times, and health fields to the IoT platform console (for example AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, or Google Cloud IoT) for the same period. Verify API polling intervals and that offline rules match your operational definition.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (devices with gap), Single value (devices offline), Line chart (connectivity rate).

## SPL

```spl
index=iot sourcetype="iot:telemetry"
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen by device_id, gateway
| eval gap_sec=now()-last_seen
| where gap_sec > 3600
| table device_id, gateway, last_seen, gap_sec
```

## Visualization

Table (devices with gap), Single value (devices offline), Line chart (connectivity rate).
